attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of worsening lower back pain and feeling generally unwell with intermittent fevers. He has a medical history of chronic lower back pain from a work-related injury. He does not take any regular medications. He denies any numbness, muscle weakness, or bladder or bowel problems.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 98/min, blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.4°C. There is marked tenderness over the L4–L5 vertebral processes. Neurological examination of the lower limbs is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features concerning for spinal infection (epidural abscess or vertebral osteomyelitis), including new severe back pain, fever, and focal vertebral tenderness. Early diagnosis is crucial as neurological deficits can develop rapidly.

MRI lumbar spine with contrast is the gold-standard investigation for diagnosing spinal infections. It provides superior soft tissue contrast and can detect epidural abscesses, vertebral osteomyelitis, and associated complications such as cord compression. Gadolinium enhancement helps delineate the extent of infection and distinguish abscess from other pathologies.

Plain lumbar spine X-ray has poor sensitivity for early spinal infection. Changes such as vertebral end-plate erosion and disc space narrowing only become apparent after several weeks of infection.

CT spine, while better than plain radiographs, lacks the soft tissue resolution of MRI and may miss early infection or small epidural collections. It is more useful for detecting bony destruction in later stages of infection.

PET scan can be useful for detecting metastatic infection foci but is not the first-line investigation for suspected spinal infection due to limited availability and poor anatomical detail.

CT-guided biopsy is typically performed after imaging confirms an infection, to obtain microbiological samples for targeted antimicrobial therapy. It is not a primary diagnostic tool.
learningPoints: MRI with gadolinium is the gold standard imaging modality for diagnosing spinal epidural abscess and vertebral osteomyelitis.
Fever and focal spine tenderness in a patient with back pain should raise suspicion for spinal infection.
Early diagnosis of spinal infection is crucial as neurological deficits may develop rapidly.
references: Sendi P, Bregenzer T, Zimmerli W. Spinal epidural abscess in clinical practice. QJM. 2007;101(1):1–12. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17982180/

Lee YJ, Sadigh S, Mankad K, Kapse N, Rajeswaran G. The imaging of osteomyelitis. Quantitative Imaging in Medicine and Surgery. 2016;6(2):184–98. Available at: https://qims.amegroups.org/article/view/9839/html

Kouijzer IJ, Scheper H, de Rooy JW, Bloem JL, Janssen MJ, van den Hoven L, et al. The diagnostic value of 18F–FDG-PET/CT and MRI in suspected vertebral osteomyelitis – a prospective study. European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging. 2017;45(5):798–805. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29256136/
